mappings.rationaleAI: Propositional and predicate logic directly cover the propositional calculus (A.1) and predicate calculus (A.2). Modal logic treatment addresses necessity, possibility, and contingency (A.4). Mathematical proofs and axioms connect to metalogic (B). Examination of the slippery slope fallacy and vagueness relates to analysis of logical fallacies (C.1.b).

Godel's incompleteness theorems demonstrating limits of axiomatization for arithmetic relate to set theory and natural-number arithmetic (A.5). The theorems directly address formal mathematical systems and the discovery of incompleteness and undecidability (B.3).

Foundational concepts of number theory, mathematical proof, and the philosophical underpinnings of mathematical truth relate to set theory and natural-number arithmetic (A.5) and the nature of formal systems (B).

Russell's Principia Mathematica and theory of descriptions contribute to the propositional calculus (A.1), predicate calculus (A.2), and set theory (A.5). His work on the foundations of mathematics connects to metalogic (B).
The Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus and logical atomism relate to propositional logic (A.1), and Wittgenstein's engagement with the foundations of mathematics connects to metalogic (B).

Analytic philosophy's methods of logical analysis and rigorous argumentation relate to formal logic broadly (A), and applications to critical thinking and everyday reasoning connect to the critique of forms of reasoning (C.1).
Aristotle's foundational contributions to formal logic, including syllogistic and the logic of predicates, directly address the predicate calculus (A.2) and the theory of the syllogism (A.3).

Leibniz's characteristica universalis and contributions to logic relate to formal logic broadly (A), his theory of possible worlds connects to modal logic (A.4), and his foundational mathematical work connects to metalogic (B).

Rhetoric as the art of persuasion, covering its theory and practice across cultures, directly addresses the theory of argumentation and the new rhetoric (C.1.a) within applied logic's critique of forms of reasoning (C.1).

Epistemological investigation of knowledge, belief, and justification, including the causal theory of knowledge and externalism, directly addresses epistemic logic dealing with concepts of belief, knowledge, and doubt (C.2).

Cognitive psychology of reasoning, deductive logic, cognitive biases, and decision-making relates to the critique of forms of reasoning (C.1), logical fallacies (C.1.b), and practical logic dealing with choosing and planning (C.3).

mappings.vsiAuthor: Emma Borg and Sarah A. Fisher
mappings.rationaleAI: Formal semantics, truth, and the philosophical analysis of meaning connect to model theory and the study of interpretations that satisfy formal systems (B.5).
Mathematical and computational foundations of information including measurement in bits, probability, and entropy relate to logics of physical application (C.4).

The Turing machine model of computation and computational complexity connect to decidability and undecidability in formal mathematical systems (B.3), and computational approaches relate to logics of physical application (C.4).

AI's foundational connection to Turing's computational theory and machine consciousness debates relate to logics of physical application (C.4) in applying logical frameworks to computing and cognition.
Properties and relationships of counting numbers, prime numbers, and divisibility connect to set theory and natural-number arithmetic (A.5).
Rigorous foundations of calculus through convergence, limits, and proof-based methods connect broadly to metalogic (B) and the formal study of mathematical systems.
Treatment of infinite sets, sequences, and paradoxes of infinity relates to set theory and natural-number arithmetic (A.5) and to metalogical questions about formal mathematical systems (B).
Rational choice theory as a formal framework for decision-making under certainty, risk, and uncertainty directly addresses practical logic dealing with choosing and planning (C.3).

Combinatorics as concerned with arrangements and counting within discrete systems, including connections to graph theory and algorithmic applications, relates to set theory and natural-number arithmetic (A.5).
